title: Rate-Amplifying Demand and the Excess Sensitivity of Long-Term Rates

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (No. 810, revised April 2021) by Hanson, Lucca, and Wright documents that long-term nominal interest rates exhibit excess sensitivity to high-frequency movements in short-term rates, and that this sensitivity has strengthened since 2000. The researchers propose a model where declines in short rates trigger rate-amplifying demand shifts in long-term bonds that are met with limited and slow arbitrage, causing long rates to temporarily overreact to short rate changes.
key_points: Long-term rates show excess sensitivity to high-frequency (daily or monthly) movements in short-term rates, and this sensitivity has grown stronger since 2000
The association between low-frequency changes (6 or 12 month horizons) in long and short rates has weakened substantially post-2000
This pattern arises because short rate increases temporarily raise the term premium component of long-term yields
Rate-amplifying demand is traced to three sources: mortgage refinancing activity, investors who extrapolate recent short rate changes, and investors who reach for yield when short rates fall
The arbitrage response to demand shifts is both limited and slow, allowing long rates to temporarily overreact
The paper discusses implications for event-study methodologies and monetary policy transmission
why_it_matters: This research matters for understanding bond market microstructure and monetary policy effectiveness. The finding that long-term rates overreact to short-term rate changes due to investor behavior (reach for yield, extrapolation) and mortgage refinancing activity suggests that standard monetary policy transmission models may be incomplete. For traders and researchers, this has implications for how to interpret rate move events and the validity of event-study approaches.
market_structure_context: The paper directly addresses market structure by examining how investor demand behavior (rate-amplifying demand from mortgage refinancing, yield extrapolation, and reach-for-yield) affects term premium dynamics and creates excess sensitivity in long-term rates. The limited and slow arbitrage response to demand shifts is a market microstructure finding with implications for bond market efficiency.
